subtopics: Classic Binary Search $\cdot$ Rotated Arrays $\cdot$ Binary Search on Answer $\cdot$ Median of Two Arrays

blocks.content: **Binary Search.** Given a sorted array and a target, return the index of target or $-1$ if not found. Required complexity: $O(\log n)$.
**Search Insert Position.** Given a sorted array and a target, return the index where target is found, or the index where it would be inserted to keep the array sorted.
**Search a 2-D Matrix.** Each row is sorted left-to-right; the first element of each row is greater than the last element of the previous row. Search for target in $O(\log(mn))$.
**Search in Rotated Sorted Array.** A sorted array has been rotated at an unknown pivot. Find target in $O(\log n)$.
**Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array.** Find the minimum element of a rotated sorted array in $O(\log n)$.
**Koko Eating Bananas.** $n$ piles of bananas; Koko can eat $k$ bananas/hour. Find the minimum $k$ such that all bananas can be eaten in $h$ hours.
**Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days.** Find the minimum ship weight capacity to ship all packages within \texttt{days} days (packages must be shipped in order).
**Split Array Largest Sum.** Split \texttt{nums} into $k$ non-empty subarrays to minimise the largest subarray sum.
**Median of Two Sorted Arrays.** Find the median of two sorted arrays \texttt{nums1} and \texttt{nums2} in $O(\log(\min(m,n)))$ time.
blocks.solution:
**Key idea: binary search on the partition.**

We want to split \texttt{nums1} at position $i$ and \texttt{nums2} at position $j = \lfloor(m+n)/2\rfloor - i$ such that: \begin{itemize}\item All elements left of the partition $\leq$ all elements right.

\item The two left halves together have size $\lfloor(m+n)/2\rfloor$.\end{itemize}

The valid partition satisfies: \texttt{nums1[i-1] <= nums2[j]} and \texttt{nums2[j-1] <= nums1[i]}.

Binary search on $i\in[0,m]$ (binary search on the shorter array).

\begin{lstlisting}
def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1: list[int], nums2: list[int]) -> float:
    # Ensure nums1 is the shorter array
    if len(nums1) > len(nums2):
        nums1, nums2 = nums2, nums1
    m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2)
    half = (m + n) // 2
    lo, hi = 0, m

    while True:
        i = (lo + hi) // 2      # partition in nums1
        j = half - i            # partition in nums2

        # Left and right boundary values (use -inf/+inf at edges)
        l1 = nums1[i-1] if i > 0 else float('-inf')
        r1 = nums1[i]   if i < m else float('inf')
        l2 = nums2[j-1] if j > 0 else float('-inf')
        r2 = nums2[j]   if j < n else float('inf')

        if l1 <= r2 and l2 <= r1:          # valid partition found
            if (m + n) % 2:
                return float(min(r1, r2))  # odd total: smaller of right halves
            return (max(l1, l2) + min(r1, r2)) / 2  # even total
        elif l1 > r2:
            hi = i - 1                     # move left in nums1
        else:
            lo = i + 1                     # move right in nums1
\end{lstlisting}

**Complexity:** $O(\log(\min(m,n)))$ time, $O(1)$ space.

**Intuition:** the median splits the combined array into two equal halves. We binary search for the partition in \texttt{nums1} (the shorter one). Once the left halves of both arrays contain exactly $\lfloor(m+n)/2\rfloor$ elements and the cross-partition conditions hold, the median is determined by the boundary elements.

**Edge cases:** \begin{itemize}\item Empty partition ($i=0$ or $j=0$): use $-\infty$ as the left boundary.

\item Full partition ($i=m$ or $j=n$): use $+\infty$ as the right boundary.

\item Odd total: median $= \min(r_{1}, r_{2})$ (smaller of right boundaries).

\item Even total: median $= (\max(l_{1},l_{2})+\min(r_{1},r_{2}))/2$.\end{itemize}

**Key insight:** this is a binary search on the partition *index*, not on a value. The “feasibility” condition is \texttt{l1 <= r2 and l2 <= r1}. Unlike the other problems in this session, the search space is positions (0 to $m$), not values.
